title: Palmiers
subtitle: Caramelised puff pastry biscuits
excerpt: Palmiers are made from puff pastry and sugar: the pastry is rolled in caster sugar, folded into its characteristic butterfly shape, sliced, and baked until the sugar caramelises. They need no mixing bowl and no equipment beyond a rolling pin.
sourceNotes: Palmiers (also called palmier biscuits, elephant ears, or palm leaves) appear in French patisserie records from the nineteenth century as a standard use for puff pastry trimmings. The form is documented across French culinary literature including Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903). The same preparation appears in British and American baking under the names elephant ears and palm leaves, all using the same double-fold method.

recipe.batchNotes: Bake one tray at a time for even caramelisation.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten the day they are baked. The caramel softens in an airtight container.

content.text: The key to palmiers is keeping the pastry cold throughout. A warm pastry log will squash when sliced and the layers will compress rather than opening into the palm shape in the oven. After shaping the log, 20 minutes in the fridge firms it enough to slice cleanly. Bake at high heat: the sugar needs to caramelise quickly, not slowly dry. Watch from the 10-minute mark; the difference between golden and burnt is about 90 seconds.

Scatter 40 g of the sugar over a clean work surface. Unroll the cold pastry sheet onto the sugar and press down lightly. Scatter the remaining sugar (and the salt if using) evenly over the top of the pastry. Roll lightly with a rolling pin to press the sugar into the pastry on both sides. Fold both long edges of the pastry inward to meet in the centre. Then fold one half over the other as if closing a book, to make a long double-folded log.

Wrap the log and refrigerate for 20 minutes. Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Slice the log into rounds about 1 cm thick. Place on the trays, cut side down, spaced 5 cm apart: they spread considerably and the caramel spreads further. Bake for 12 to 15 minutes, until deep amber on the underside. Carefully flip each palmier with a palette knife and bake for a further 3 to 4 minutes to caramelise the other side. Transfer immediately to a cooling rack; if left on the tray the caramel sets solid and sticks.
